title: About
text:
I am Sophia Sufei Chen, a final year Business Studies student at Dublin City University, specialising in Business Analytics and Marketing Planning. This ePortfolio presents my academic background, technical skills, and professional development in a clear and professional format.
I am particularly interested in business analytics, customer insight, and using data-informed thinking to support better business decisions in real organisational settings.

title: About
text:
Background

Academic and Professional Development
My background combines university study with practical experience in customer service, retail operations, and accounting support. These experiences have helped me develop strong communication, teamwork, organisation, and problem-solving skills in professional environments.
Through my studies, I have also developed growing confidence in business systems, analytics tools, and data-driven decision-making. I am especially interested in how businesses can improve performance through better use of information, digital tools, and customer understanding.

title: Project 1 — Park & Polish
text:
Marketing
Excel
SQL
Market analysis, structured business planning, and analytical thinking supported by Excel and SQL-informed logic.


This project focuses on the Park & Polish business concept and explores how a business idea can be developed through structured market analysis, service planning, and commercial positioning. The work allowed me to think about how a new business could identify market opportunities, understand competitors, and communicate value in a practical and visually organised way.
A key strength of this project is that it connects creative business thinking with analytical structure. I used Excel-based planning, comparison logic, and SQL-informed reasoning to support a more evidence-based and professional approach to the business concept.



To evaluate the business idea, I focused on four areas:

customer need and service demand
competitor positioning
pricing and service structure
operational feasibility

Excel supported the organisation of service categories, simple comparisons, and planning assumptions. SQL-informed thinking also influenced the way I structured information, grouped comparisons, and considered how business data could later be queried and analysed more formally.











Area
Example Question
Business Value




Customer demand
What service would customers use most often?
helps shape service focus


Competitor review
How do similar businesses position themselves?
supports differentiation


Pricing logic
Which package structure is easiest to understand?
improves customer clarity


Operations
What resources are needed to deliver the service?
supports feasibility



 



This project taught me that a business concept becomes much stronger when it is supported by structured analysis rather than only creativity. I learned to think more carefully about market relevance, practical implementation, and how to organise information clearly for presentation.



This project helped me strengthen my ability to connect business creativity with more structured planning and analytical thinking. I learned how important it is to move beyond an idea itself and instead assess whether it is realistic, competitive, and relevant in a real market context. I also became more aware of the importance of presenting business information clearly, because good structure and visual communication can make an idea much easier to understand.
Through this project, I gained confidence in approaching business concepts in a more practical and commercially minded way.



The main skills I developed through this project include market analysis, competitor review, business planning, and visual presentation. I also strengthened my confidence in using Excel and structured analytical logic to support business ideas. In addition, the project improved my ability to organise information, identify key findings, and communicate insights in a professional way. These skills are directly relevant to future roles in business analysis, planning, and marketing-related work.

title: Project 2 — Power BI
text:
Power BI
Business Intelligence
Visualisation
Dashboard design, business storytelling, and performance visualisation using Power BI.


This project focuses on business dashboard development and visual communication using Power BI. It involved presenting business performance data in a way that is clear, interactive, and suitable for decision-making. The dashboard was designed to highlight key business indicators such as sales, costs, net profit, and category performance, while also making the information visually accessible for a wider audience.
This project is important in my portfolio because it demonstrates my ability to move from raw business data toward organised visual insight. It also shows how data presentation plays a key role in helping people understand performance and identify patterns.



 
These dashboard screens show how business data can be summarised visually to support comparison, interpretation, and decision-making.



The dashboard was designed to summarise business performance across categories and support quick comparison of key measures. I focused on selecting the most meaningful indicators, organising them into a clear layout, and presenting trends in a visually readable way. This process strengthened my understanding of how dashboard structure affects interpretation.



The Python example below demonstrates a simple category-level comparison that supports dashboard interpretation and business reporting.


Code
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = pd.DataFrame({
    "Category": ["Travel", "Construction", "Electronics", "Finance", "Retail"],
    "Net_Profit": [82, 76, -14, 58, 41]
})

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.bar(data["Category"], data["Net_Profit"])
plt.title("Net Profit by Category")
plt.xlabel("Category")
plt.ylabel("Net Profit")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Example Python visualisation of category-level business performance




This example shows how categories can be compared visually through executable code. It supports the wider project theme of turning business data into clear and useful insight.



A key learning point from this project was that visualisation is not only about making data attractive. It is about helping users notice patterns, compare results, and make decisions more efficiently. I learned that the choice of chart, layout, and metric all influence how useful a dashboard becomes.



This project helped me appreciate the value of visual storytelling in business analysis. I learned that a dashboard is not just about displaying charts, but about organising information in a way that makes insight easier to recognise and interpret. It also showed me the importance of design choices, layout, and selecting the most useful metrics for a business audience.
Working on this project improved my confidence in balancing analytical content with presentation quality, which is an important skill in modern business and reporting environments.



Through this project, I developed stronger skills in Power BI, dashboard design, visual business reporting, and data presentation. I also improved my ability to identify useful business measures and think about what information decision-makers need most. In addition, the project strengthened my sense of layout, communication, and professional presentation. These are valuable skills for business intelligence, reporting, and analytical roles where visual clarity matters.

title: Project 3: Risk Management
text:
This project focuses on risk management principles and how organisations identify, assess, and respond to different types of risk. It explores how structured analysis can support better decision-making and reduce uncertainty in business environments.




identify key business risks
evaluate likelihood and impact
compare risk priority levels
discuss mitigation strategies
reflect on the importance of structured risk planning




  
These visuals support the discussion by showing how risk-related information can be structured, compared, and interpreted in a more systematic way.



The table below presents a simple example of how business risks can be assessed using likelihood and impact scores.



Risk
Likelihood (1-5)
Impact (1-5)
Risk Score




Cash flow pressure
4
5
20


Supply delay
3
4
12


Data error
4
3
12


Staff shortage
3
3
9


Reputational issue
2
5
10






#| echo: true #| code-fold: true #| fig-cap: “Illustrative risk score comparison”
import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
risk_data = pd.DataFrame({ “Risk”: [“Cash flow pressure”, “Supply delay”, “Data error”, “Staff shortage”, “Reputational issue”], “Risk_Score”: [20, 12, 12, 9, 10] })
plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5)) plt.bar(risk_data[“Risk”], risk_data[“Risk_Score”]) plt.title(“Illustrative Business Risk Scores”) plt.xlabel(“Risk Type”) plt.ylabel(“Risk Score”) plt.xticks(rotation=20, ha=“right”) plt.tight_layout() plt.show()
This chart demonstrates how risks can be ranked visually once likelihood and impact have been assessed. It supports prioritisation by making higher-risk areas easier to identify.



Risk management is important because it helps organisations reduce uncertainty and prepare for operational, financial, and strategic challenges. A good risk management framework improves decision making and supports long-term sustainability. In this example, cash flow pressure appears as the highest-priority issue because it combines high likelihood with high impact.




critical analysis
structured reporting
business judgement
visual communication
risk prioritisation




This project helped me understand how risk can affect decision-making and why organisations need clear frameworks to manage uncertainty effectively. It also showed me that risk management is not only theoretical. When risks are organised, scored, and interpreted clearly, they become much easier to discuss and manage in a practical business context.

title: Reflection
text:
Reflection
Building this ePortfolio helped me understand that technical skills, presentation, and professional identity are closely connected. I learned not only how to organise content online, but also how to communicate my strengths more clearly and professionally.

Git and Version Control
One of the most important things I learned from this module was the value of Git and version control. At the beginning, Git felt mainly like a technical requirement, but through practice I came to understand how useful it is for managing progress, correcting mistakes, and keeping a clear record of development. Regular commits helped me track improvements across the homepage, project pages, navigation, and styling, and made it easier to recover earlier versions when something went wrong.


Quarto and Website Development
Developing the ePortfolio with Quarto helped me understand how website structure, content, and presentation work together. I learned how to build and manage pages, use navigation effectively, and improve usability through layout, styling, and organisation. I also learned that a professional site is not only about appearance, but also about clarity, accessibility, and how easily visitors can understand the content.


Python and Project Development
Through the projects in this portfolio, I developed a stronger understanding of how Python can support analysis and visualisation. Even when the code examples were relatively simple, they helped me see the value of executable analysis compared with static screenshots or explanations alone. I became more confident in using structured logic, reading outputs, and thinking about how code can support business and analytical interpretation.


Challenges and Problem Solving
A major challenge during this assignment was making sure that the portfolio worked correctly as a live website while also keeping the design professional and organised. I had to solve issues related to file paths, page rendering, YAML formatting, navigation links, and layout consistency. For example, when pages disappeared from the site or Quarto failed to render, I had to identify whether the problem came from the file itself, the navbar configuration, or the output folder. This taught me to troubleshoot more systematically and to test changes carefully before publishing.


Future Goals
This ePortfolio has shown me that skills in Git, Quarto, Python, and web presentation are useful beyond this module. In the future, I want to continue improving in business analysis, finance support, and digital communication. I also want to develop stronger technical confidence in Python, SQL, and business intelligence tools. More importantly, I want to keep building a professional profile that combines analytical ability, practical learning, and effective presentation.

title: More About Me
text:
I have developed a growing interest in advanced Python learning as part of my academic and professional development. Python has become one of the most valuable tools in my studies because it helps me move beyond basic theory and work more practically with data, logic, and problem-solving. Through learning Python, I have improved my understanding of data cleaning, simple automation, structured analysis, and presenting results in a more efficient way.
Python is especially important to me because it connects strongly with business analytics. It supports the way I think through problems step by step and encourages accuracy, structure, and logical reasoning. As I continue learning, I hope to strengthen my confidence in applying Python to more business-focused tasks, including analysis, reporting, and decision support.





Learning Python has helped me understand how analytical thinking can be applied in a practical and structured way. I have become more comfortable with logic, syntax, and breaking down problems into manageable steps. This has supported my wider development in business analytics and strengthened my confidence in working with data-based tasks.






Advanced Python learning also represents personal progress in my technical development. As I continue learning, I am improving both my problem-solving approach and my confidence with digital tools. It is a skill that supports my academic work now and will also be valuable in future business and analytical roles.

title: More About Me
text:
Hobbies: Pilates and Swimming
Outside of study and work, I value hobbies that help me stay healthy, focused, and balanced. Pilates and swimming are two of the activities I enjoy most. They allow me to maintain both physical wellbeing and mental clarity, which is important when balancing university work, internship responsibilities, and professional development. I find that regular movement helps me manage stress more effectively and stay disciplined in other areas of life.
These hobbies also reflect qualities that are important to me more broadly: consistency, self-improvement, and resilience. Both Pilates and swimming require patience and steady progress, and I enjoy that sense of routine and personal development. For me, these activities are not only hobbies but also part of maintaining a productive and positive lifestyle.




Pilates
Pilates helps me improve flexibility, posture, balance, and concentration. I enjoy it because it combines physical movement with control and discipline. It is a useful way for me to stay active while also building focus and consistency, which are qualities that support both my studies and my work.





Swimming
Swimming is another activity that I enjoy because it helps me feel refreshed, energised, and mentally clear. It gives me time to reset and maintain a healthy routine. I value swimming not only as exercise, but also as a way to support overall wellbeing and balance in a busy academic and professional schedule.
